item.title: It’s my cake day and here’s the 1st thing I favorited (as is customary) and I can even add a story!
item.description: Funnily enough, I have such a story myself. I’m German and lived in England for 9months in 2004/5 as a foreign language assistant. I’d gone out for a walk (the English sure love their walks :) ), had sat on a bench, when along came this elderly gentleman with his dog. He bade me good day, I replied politely, and he obviously recognized my accent. It immediately threw him back 60yrs; he told me how he’d stayed in Germany as an occupation soldier, how he’d go out with German girls, he said „would you like to dance with me?“ in German, which was so darn cute, and sang part of a German folk song; but he also mentioned how he’d lain in the trenches and cursed every single German man, woman and child. It was a deeply emotional encounter; Imagine going out with German girls in 1945 after fighting a war against their people, then meeting a German girl the same age 60yrs later… I like to imagine that our encounter gave him some kind of closure on what he went through as a young man. He’s surely dead by now and I’m almost 40 with two kids. Life sure is strange, guys… Viral edit: I’m so happy these stories touch so many others! I’ve been reading every single story in the comments, which must have been an major pain to write, what with the comment limitations. And I love all of them! Every time I saw the story of the lady walking from Burma, I thought, one of these days I’m going to tell my story too. Thanks everyone for appreciating it!
item.dc:creator: Fortherea

item.title: No words
item.description: For context, I’m a nurse. My husband has been battling prostate cancer for the last year. Finally, after multiple biopsies, tests, scans, and major surgery his PSA is undetectable which means he is cancer free! Gotta say, kinda don’t appreciate my manager calling to scream at me at 6am because I can’t come in to pick up a shift, and uses my time off work to take care of my husband as a “gotcha”. And people want to know why nurses are leaving the profession in droves. Time to find a new place of employment. #cancersucks #fuckthat
item.dc:creator: nursenurseynurse7

item.title: Sad day for a lot of American schools
item.description: Awareness is not indoctrination Edit: most viral! Glad so many of you feel the same way. I probably should add that the most shocking ban was announced today: a graphic novel called “Maus” by Art Spiegelman that depicts the Holocaust—apparently it encourages “snitching on your countrymen?” Talk about some BS. Extra Sauce: https://abcnews.go.com/amp/US/pulitzer-prize-winning-holocaust-book-maus-joins-list/story?id=82508346
item.dc:creator: boobsoffury

item.title: Day 244: of cheeseburgers I want inside of me
item.description: The Cheesy Todd Beef Patty, American Cheese, Lettuce, Tomato, Red Onion And Pickle Served On Two Bacon-Jalapeño Deep Fried Mac N’ Cheese “Buns" From Datz In Tampa, FL Previous Day 243: https://imgur.com/gallery/jtupf14 MESSAGE ME BURGER PICS OR YOUR FAVORITE BURGER SPOTS! Map of all burger spots that are available on yelp! Big thanks to @OhBoyDoIHaveNews4U for this. Link to his post and map https://imgur.com/gallery/vcObDuD

item.title: Work of Auschwitz Survivor & Artist Jan Komsky (Warning: Disturbing images)
item.description: Komsky was a member of the Polish Underground who was on the first train to Auschwitz, and was Prisoner number 564. On December 29, 1942, he escaped from Auschwitz with several other members of the underground and hid out in Broszkowice, in the same building as a senior SS Officer. He then went to Cracow where, just 16 days after escaping, he was caught and arrested, (under a different name) and was sent back to Auschwitz (under a different name). He survived Auschwitz, and post war in the DP Camps he used his time to draw and paint dozens of pieces of what he witnessed with his own eyes. Remember these Images, this is what happened. When you make Nazi Comparisons, think of these and think, it this remotely similar. To learn more, watch the documentary Eyewitness (1999) about his life and work.
item.dc:creator: Hirschtory

item.title: Holocaust Memorial Day - for the Children
item.description:
The drawing “Earth, from the Moon” was created by Petr Ginz (b. 1 Feb 1928) sometime between 1942-44, when he was 14-16. It captures the loneliness and isolation of an inquisitive, intelligent child stolen from their parents by the nonsense logic of the Holocaust. Petr was an inmate in Theresienstadt concentration camp, an unusual concentration camp with a high proportion of notable Jewish cultural figures. This was used by the Nazi Regime as a “Potemkin village” for viewing by international aid organisations such as the Red Cross, to demonstrate the humane conditions of Nazi ‘resettlement’ of Jews. In 1944, it was the subject of a 90 minute propaganda “documentary” about the happy conditions of the Jews living there, as part of an abortive effort to discredit accurate reports of the Holocaust. In order to make it appear more homely to outsiders, the camp had an amount of cultural and artistic supplies unlike anything else in the Nazi camp system. It remained, however, always a transit camp, just a prettified stop on the way to the gas chambers, of the 140,000 who came to it 120,000 died.
[Picture: Petr Ginz in 1939/40. The posters behind him are advertising the food-providing work of the National Socialist People's Welfare organisation, a Nazi state-backed charitable group focused on children, it was responsible for the evacuation of German children from cities threatened by strategic bombing.] Petr Ginz was abducted from his family in 1942 when he turned 14, as he was the child of a Jewish man and a non-Jewish woman. He was already forced to wear a Star of David, noting in his diary he counted “sixty-nine “sheriffs”” like him on the way to school. The Nazi authorities in occupied Czechoslovakia (part of which was turned into the Protectorate of Bohemia and Moravia) ​unlike in Germany itself, had decided to take punitive action against these “mischlings” (“mutts”). Later, his father and sister were sent to Theresienstadt camp, but survived until the end of the war. While in Theresienstadt Petr Ginz was placed in Home No. 1 (building L417) with about 100 other boys, part of a series of children’s homes organised by nationality and age by the Jewish authorities of Theresienstadt for unaccompanied children. Petr was a remarkably intelligent boy, who spoke Czech and Esperanto (a result of his parents being part of the Utopian Esperanto movement), and had wrote and illustrated four storybooks by the time he was abducted. One, a Jules Verne-style adventure called “A visit from Prehistory” survives to this day. In Home 1, Petr Ginz continued to learn, thanks to Theresienstadt having a relatively extensive library of confiscated books, and thanks to secretive schooling from former teachers and students in the camp population – such teaching was, if discovered, punished severely. Petr probably gained some knowledge of Hebrew from these classes. Home 1’s symbol was a Vernsian rocketship flying above a book to a star.
[Picture: the logo of Home 1, and possibly the Republic of Škid.] Most evocatively, encouraged by his secret teacher, 29 year old “Professor” Valtr Eisinger, Petr became the driving force behind a magazine of stories, art, poems, and jokes wrote and drawn by the children of Home 1. “Vedem” - “we lead”, akin to “we’re winning!” or, as modern children say when they do a great clutch on Minecraft, “Winning!”. The boys named Home 1 “The Republic of Škid”, designed a flag, instituted an award honourific (“Geroj” – Hero), and wrote an anthem. Škid was an acronym of “Skola Imeni Dostoyevskovo” - “The School of Dostoevsky” – the existentialist Russian author of ‘Crime & Punishment’, ‘The Brothers Karamazov’ and other great works - this school appeared in a 1926 Russian novel-memoir called "The Republic of ShKID", it was a home for homeless children of the Russian Civil War in Petrograd - today St Petersburg. A film version of the 1926 novel was released in 1966 in the USSR. The triple bunks of the boys formed a ‘square’ around an open area, so the boys decorated the heads of the beds with paintings of shop fronts, depicting Wenceslas Square, Prague, including the front of the National Museum. (In the 1980s, the Communist regime was to re-pave Wenceslas square with stolen and smashed Jewish headstones.) The children of Home 1 produced 83 single-copy issues of Vedem, including submissions from other ‘homes’, which were then read out to the young population of Theresienstadt. About 800 pages survive today thanks to a survivor, Zdenĕk/Sydney Taussig (aged 91 in 2021), who buried them. They represent an incredible and intimate view into the minds of children knowing they were living with the spectre of extermination hovering over them.
[Picture: The first issue of 1943 celebrates the New Year in style.] The identity of many contributors is unknown, as they generally stuck to the classic satirical magazine style of using pseudonyms, such as "Dummy" or "Bolshevik." Contributors whose identities we know include Jiří Grünbaum, who called himself variously "Medic Šnajer," "Socialist Šnajer," or "Šnajer”. Kurt Kotouč went by “The Saint”. Hanuš Kominík went by “Švejk”, seemingly a reference to the celebrated 1928 Czech novel ‘The Good Soldier Švejk’, George “Jiři” Brady was “Bear Brady;” Herbert Fischl was “Don Herberto;” Hanus Beck was “Primadonna Beck;” and Jiři Bruml was “Abscess”. At some point in 1943, ten of the most prolific contributors began to refer to themselves as the "Academy." One unknown contributor – “Embryo”, provided a ‘quote of the week’: “Football is the best game, right after Monopoly.” The magic and possibility of science fiction & space travel appears a great deal in Vedem, including in a poem by Hanus Hachenberg (b. 12 June 1929), who wrote the excerpt below when he was 12-14. “I was once a little child, Three years ago. That child who longed for other worlds. But now I am no more a child For I have learned to hate. I am a grown-up person now, I have known fear.” Hanus was murdered on 18 December 1943, at Auschwitz, aged 14.
[Picture: three of the Vedem contributors. Hanuš Pollak, Herbert Fischl, & Jiři Bruml] 18 year old Hanuš Pollak submitted “Negroes and Us” to Vedem after reading “Uncle Tom’s Cabin”, comparing the situation of Jews in the camp to American slaves “Just like the negroes…we bear our fate calmly and heroically, looking it straight in the eye”. He was murdered at Auschwitz on 6 September 1943. Jiří Grünbaum (Šnajer) was also murdered at Auschwitz on 6 September 1943, aged 13. Hopefully with his fellow writer Pollak to hold his hand. Hanuš Kominík (Švejk) was murdered at Auschwitz on 6 October 1944, fifteen days into being 15 years old. Herbert Fischl (Don Herberto) was murdered at Auschwitz on 28 September 1944, aged 16, alongside him was former child actor Hanus Beck (Primadonna Beck), who was 15. Jiři Bruml (Abscess) was murdered at Auschwitz on 15 May 1944, aged 14. “Professor” Valtr Eisinger was shot by the SS during a death march from Buchenwald concentration camp in January 1945, aged 30/31. George Brady (Bear Brady) and Kurt Kotouč (The Saint) survived.
[Picture: Vedem was not the only magazine published by children in Theresienstadt, “Rim Rim Rim” was made by the boys of Home 7, who called themselves “the Hawks”, they were able to produce multiple copies of one issue, and made special issues for children leaving the camp “to go to the East”. “Bonaco” was produced by the teenage girls of Home 11.] Petr Ginz’s diaries were discovered in unclear circumstances in an attic in 2003, confirmed as genuine by Petr’s younger sister, the two books made of scrap paper bound together with string provide an agonising look into a child-near-teenager growing in such conditions, grasping at the world beyond the wire: “May 1944 I finished the notes from Ceylon and bound them. I completed the preparatory work for the study of general education: I have read The System of Sociology by Chalupny, which contains a classification of sciences, and in this framework I have made a plan to learn a little about each science. … I have read: Gwen Bristow: Deep Summer. Franck: Without a Penny Around the World, the detective novel The Silky Face, Chalupny: The System of Sociology, Wells: A Short History of the World, Pedagogical newsletter, Jiri Vaija: The Storytellers. … I have drawn: The Brewery." Petr Ginz was murdered with Zyklon B by the Nazis at Auschwitz II Birkenau on 29 September 1944, aged 16. Of the 92 boys involved in creating Vedem, 15 survived.
[Picture: The Space Shuttle Columbia disaster, 2003.] In 2003, Israeli astronaut Ilan Ramon was asked by his mother, an Auschwitz survivor, to take something into space to commemorate the Holocaust, and he chose “Earth, from the Moon”, a copy of which was provided by the Yad Vashem Museum, prepared to specifications provided by NASA. Ilan Ramon went into space aboard the space shuttle Columbia, which broke up during re-entry on 1 February 2003, killing all its crew. A new copy of the picture returned to space on March 21, 2018, taken to the International Space Station in the care of American astronaut Andrew Feustel, a member of Max Q, the all-astronaut band. The original artwork is maintained & kept at the Yad Vashem Museum. Had Petr Ginz not been murdered by the Nazis, when 39-year old Neil Armstrong stepped on to the moon in 1969, Petr would have been 41.
[Picture: Andrew Feustel with the picture in the ISS, looking down on a view Petr Ginz was only allowed to imagine.] Every person is our brother, Whether Christian or a Jew. Proudly we are marching forward The Republic of Škid is me and you. - Anthem of the Republic of Škid, 1942-45 ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ More Holocaust history: America's forgotten victims of the Holocaust: https://imgur.com/gallery/jVmVfvV Roma: https://imgur.com/gallery/Ck9bBbo An artist, during & after: https://imgur.com/gallery/kHvqtSX Orphans after the war: https://imgur.com/gallery/ERfnPEn Imprisoned by Stalin & Hitler: https://imgur.com/gallery/o7nI7UG LGBT people and the Holocaust: https://imgur.com/gallery/xIbrOJW The hunt for gay history destroyed by Nazis: https://imgur.com/gallery/Goiunle Mentally ill & disabled people and the Holocaust: https://imgur.com/gallery/fb4IEAk Photographing the victims: https://imgur.com/gallery/YKywOzU Kastner: https://imgur.com/gallery/yxsDfew Sugihara: https://imgur.com/gallery/vdW86Xb Perl: https://imgur.com/gallery/je5JkQd Bruno & Otto: https://imgur.com/gallery/t9d4ToO Rumkowski: https://imgur.com/gallery/sG3ywZF Coward: https://imgur.com/gallery/hHVzpIu Gerstein: https://imgur.com/gallery/pNQepwe
item.dc:creator: BodicaRexVII
